content.text: Knowledge Base Content Review and Freshness Cadence

A GenAI service's knowledge base accumulates content over its lifetime: API reference documentation, troubleshooting guides, usage tutorials, FAQ articles, safety policy documents. Without a structured review cadence, this content decays. Error messages change, API parameters are renamed, behavioral quirks are patched, and the documentation continues to describe the old behavior — misleading users and generating support tickets that the current documentation should have prevented.

Content freshness is not simply about running a scheduled sweep. Different content types decay at different rates: API documentation can become incorrect within hours of a deployment; conceptual guides may remain accurate for months. An effective review cadence matches the review frequency to the content's decay risk.

attrs.alt: Knowledge base freshness cadence matrix: content types ranked by decay risk with review triggers, cadences, and owners

content.text: Content Decay and Its Sources
Content becomes stale when reality changes and the documentation does not. For a GenAI service, the sources of content decay are:
API changes
: parameter names, default values, response schemas, error codes — any code change can invalidate documentation
Model behavior changes
: fine-tuning, safety filter updates, or model version upgrades can change how the model responds to specific inputs that the documentation describes
Infrastructure changes
: rate limits, token quotas, latency SLAs, and availability targets change as the service scales
Policy and legal changes
: acceptable use policies, content moderation rules, and data retention disclosures must reflect current legal and compliance requirements
Support pattern evolution
: the questions users actually ask evolve over time as the user base grows and the product changes; FAQ articles that addressed the original user population may miss the current one
Stale documentation has a compounding cost.

A user who follows stale documentation and fails generates a support ticket. The support agent who answers the ticket (if there is no updated article) may give an incorrect answer based on the same stale content. The issue compounds until the documentation is fixed — and the time between the staleness occurring and the fix is proportional to how infrequently the content is reviewed.
Freshness Metadata

Every knowledge base article must carry freshness metadata as machine-readable fields. This enables automated staleness detection rather than relying on human memory of when content was last reviewed.
Required Fields

# Frontmatter for every KB article (markdown-based KB example)
---
title: "Using the Chat Completions API"
id: kb-api-chat-completions
content_type: api_reference          # api_reference | troubleshooting | tutorial | faq | policy | archived
created_date: "2024-03-15"
last_reviewed_date: "2025-11-01"     # REQUIRED — date of last human review
reviewed_by: "alice.chen@company.com"
review_cadence_days: 7               # How often this content should be reviewed
applies_to_version: "v2.3.x"        # API/model version this content describes
freshness_status: current            # current | review_due | stale | archived
product_area: ["api", "llm"]        # Tags for routing review responsibility
---

# Using the Chat Completions API

...
Automated Staleness Detection

# scripts/kb_freshness_check.py
# Scans all KB articles and flags those overdue for review

import os, yaml, datetime
from pathlib import Path

KB_ROOT = Path("docs/knowledge-base")
REPORT_OUTPUT = "reports/kb-freshness-report.md"

def is_review_due(article_meta: dict) -> bool:
    last_reviewed = datetime.date.fromisoformat(article_meta["last_reviewed_date"])
    cadence_days = article_meta.get("review_cadence_days", 90)
    due_date = last_reviewed + datetime.timedelta(days=cadence_days)
    return datetime.date.today() >= due_date

def staleness_level(article_meta: dict) -> str:
    last_reviewed = datetime.date.fromisoformat(article_meta["last_reviewed_date"])
    cadence_days = article_meta.get("review_cadence_days", 90)
    days_overdue = (datetime.date.today() - last_reviewed).days - cadence_days
    if days_overdue <= 0:
        return "current"
    elif days_overdue <= cadence_days:
        return "review_due"
    else:
        return "stale"  # overdue by more than one full cadence

def scan_kb_articles() -> list[dict]:
    results = []
    for md_file in KB_ROOT.rglob("*.md"):
        text = md_file.read_text()
        if not text.startswith("---"):
            continue
        # Parse YAML frontmatter
        end = text.index("---", 3)
        meta = yaml.safe_load(text[3:end])
        if "last_reviewed_date" not in meta:
            results.append({"file": str(md_file), "status": "missing_metadata"})
            continue
        level = staleness_level(meta)
        results.append({
            "file": str(md_file),
            "title": meta.get("title", "untitled"),
            "status": level,
            "last_reviewed": meta["last_reviewed_date"],
            "cadence_days": meta.get("review_cadence_days", 90),
            "reviewed_by": meta.get("reviewed_by", "unknown"),
        })
    return results

def write_report(results: list[dict]) -> None:
    stale = [r for r in results if r["status"] == "stale"]
    due = [r for r in results if r["status"] == "review_due"]
    missing = [r for r in results if r["status"] == "missing_metadata"]
    current = [r for r in results if r["status"] == "current"]

    lines = [
        "# KB Freshness Report",
        f"Generated: {datetime.date.today().isoformat()}",
        f"Total articles: {len(results)} | Current: {len(current)} | Review due: {len(due)} | Stale: {len(stale)} | Missing metadata: {len(missing)}",
        "",
    ]
    if stale:
        lines += ["## Stale (overdue by more than one review cadence — action required)"]
        for r in sorted(stale, key=lambda x: x["last_reviewed"]):
            lines.append(f"- [{r['title']}]({r['file']}) — last reviewed {r['last_reviewed']} ({r['cadence_days']}d cadence)")
    if due:
        lines += ["", "## Review Due"]
        for r in due:
            lines.append(f"- [{r['title']}]({r['file']}) — last reviewed {r['last_reviewed']}")
    if missing:
        lines += ["", "## Missing Metadata (frontmatter not present — add before next review)"]
        for r in missing:
            lines.append(f"- {r['file']}")

    Path(REPORT_OUTPUT).parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    Path(REPORT_OUTPUT).write_text("
".join(lines))
    print(f"Report written to {REPORT_OUTPUT}")
    print(f"Summary: {len(stale)} stale, {len(due)} due, {len(missing)} missing metadata")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    results = scan_kb_articles()
    write_report(results)
    # Exit non-zero if there are stale articles (for CI integration)
    stale_count = sum(1 for r in results if r["status"] == "stale")
    raise SystemExit(stale_count > 0)
Review Cadence by Content Type
API Reference Documentation — Continuous

API reference documentation decays immediately when the API changes. The only reliable approach is to generate reference documentation directly from the API definition (OpenAPI spec, protobuf definitions, JSON schema) rather than maintaining hand-authored reference docs that can diverge from the implementation.

# .github/workflows/docs-generation.yml
# Regenerates API reference docs on every merge to main
name: Generate API Docs

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - 'api/openapi.yaml'
      - 'proto/**/*.proto'

jobs:
  generate-docs:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Generate OpenAPI reference docs
        run: |
          npx @redocly/cli build-docs api/openapi.yaml             --output docs/knowledge-base/api-reference/generated.html

      - name: Update freshness metadata
        run: |
          python scripts/update_kb_metadata.py             --file docs/knowledge-base/api-reference/generated.md             --reviewed-by "ci-automation"             --cadence-days 7

      - name: Commit updated docs
        uses: stefanzweifel/git-auto-commit-action@v5
        with:
          commit_message: "docs(api): regenerate reference from OpenAPI spec"
          file_pattern: "docs/knowledge-base/api-reference/"
For content that cannot be auto-generated from the API definition — narrative descriptions, examples, context about why an endpoint works a certain way — a weekly human review is required.
Troubleshooting Guides — Monthly Plus On-Change

Troubleshooting articles are authored in response to support tickets and production incidents. They become stale when the underlying issue is fixed or when the error message changes. Two triggers drive review:
Monthly scheduled review

: a support engineer reviews all troubleshooting guides against the current support ticket volume. Articles that no longer address actual tickets are archived; new tickets that lack coverage trigger new article creation.
Change-triggered review

: any deployment that changes error codes, messages, or the behavior described in a troubleshooting guide triggers a review of the affected articles. This is implemented by tagging troubleshooting articles with the code areas they document and running a review notification when those areas change.
The change-triggered review is implemented as a CI step that maps modified code files to KB article tags:
FAQ Articles — Monthly Plus Ticket Pattern Analysis

FAQ articles are most useful when they address questions users are actually asking. A monthly review driven by support ticket analysis ensures the FAQ reflects the current user population's questions:
The Review Process
What a Review Involves
A knowledge base review is not just reading an article to see if it 'feels right'. A structured review follows a checklist:
Check
How to Verify
All code examples run correctly
Copy the example, run it against the current API, confirm the output matches what the article claims
Error codes and messages are current
Check the current error response against the documented response; look for error code registry changes
API parameter names and defaults are accurate
Compare against current OpenAPI spec or source code
Screenshots or UI references are current
If the article references a UI, verify the UI has not changed
Links are not broken
Run a link checker on internal and external links
The use case the article addresses is still relevant
Check if the feature, pattern, or error the article describes still exists in the current product
Review Workflow Integration
The review process should be integrated into the engineering workflow rather than handled as a separate, off-cycle process. Practical integration patterns:
Review assignments via GitHub issues
: the freshness check script opens a GitHub issue for each article that is due for review, assigned to the article's owner
Review as part of feature release checklist
: the deployment checklist for any feature that changes documented behavior includes a step to update affected KB articles before the release ships
Quarterly KB sprint
: one sprint per quarter has a dedicated KB review allocation; reviewers pick articles from the review-due queue

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/create_review_issues.sh
# Creates GitHub issues for KB articles that are due for review
# Run weekly via CI cron job

REPO="company/genai-service"
LABEL="kb-review"

# Run freshness check and parse output
python scripts/kb_freshness_check.py --json-output /tmp/kb_freshness.json

# Create GitHub issues for each stale or review-due article
python3 - <<'EOF'
import json, subprocess

with open("/tmp/kb_freshness.json") as f:
    results = json.load(f)

for article in results:
    if article["status"] not in ("stale", "review_due"):
        continue
    title = f"KB Review: {article['title']}"
    body = (
        "Knowledge base article is due for review.

"
        "**Article**: " + article["file"] + "
"
        "**Last reviewed**: " + article["last_reviewed"] + "
"
        "**Cadence**: " + str(article["cadence_days"]) + " days
"
        "**Status**: " + article["status"] + "

"
        "Please review this article and update the `last_reviewed_date` in its frontmatter."
    )
    subprocess.run([
        "gh", "issue", "create",
        "--repo", "company/genai-service",
        "--title", title,
        "--body", body,
        "--label", "kb-review",
        "--assignee", article.get("reviewed_by", "@me"),
    ], check=True)
    print(f"Created issue for: {article['title']}")
EOF
Measuring Knowledge Base Health
Freshness is not the only health metric for a knowledge base. A complete health picture includes:
Metric
Target
How Measured
Freshness rate
> 95% of articles with current status
kb_freshness_check.py weekly run
Self-service deflection rate
> 40% of support contacts resolved by KB without agent
Support ticket system analytics
Search zero-result rate
< 10% of KB searches return no results
KB search analytics
Article accuracy (user feedback)
> 90% of articles rated 'helpful' by users
In-article feedback widget
Time to create coverage for new issues
< 5 business days from incident to KB article
Incident-to-article gap tracking

These metrics are reviewed monthly by the support team lead and platform engineering. Declining self-service deflection rate is the most operationally significant signal: it means the knowledge base is failing to prevent support tickets, which directly increases support costs.
Summary

Knowledge base freshness is maintained through two mechanisms: automated detection of stale content (via freshness metadata and scheduled scans) and structured review processes that match cadence to decay risk. API reference documentation requires near-continuous review because it can become incorrect within hours of a code change; conceptual guides and FAQ articles can be reviewed quarterly or monthly.

The machinery that makes this work at scale is not human diligence — it is the metadata framework that makes every article's freshness status machine-readable, the automated scans that surface stale content without requiring anyone to remember to check, and the CI integration that triggers review when code changes affect documented behavior.

For GenAI services specifically, two content categories require extra attention: troubleshooting articles (because model behavior changes frequently, making the error patterns they describe obsolete) and safety and policy documents (because they carry legal and compliance implications if they fall out of sync with actual policy). Assigning explicit owners for these categories and keeping their review cadence shorter than standard content prevents the compliance gaps and support escalations that stale documentation in these areas produces.
